name: ma'at (partner in justice)
shortDescription: Ma'at is the ancient Egyptian concept of truth, balance, order, law, morality, and justice. She was the personification of these concepts and played a crucial role in the weighing of the heart ceremony in the afterlife.
description: Ma'at was the ancient Egyptian concept of cosmic order, balance, truth, law, morality, and justice. She was personified as a goddess, often depicted as a woman with an ostrich feather on her head. Ma'at was more than just a deity; she represented the fundamental principle upon which the universe was built and maintained. Her role was central to both the earthly and the afterlife realms. The pharaoh was seen as the maintainer of Ma'at on Earth, responsible for upholding justice and order. In the afterlife, the heart of the deceased was weighed against the Feather of Ma'at in the Hall of Judgement. If the heart was lighter than the feather, the deceased was deemed worthy of entering the afterlife, ruled by Osiris. If the heart was heavier, it was devoured by Ammit, the Devourer of Souls, resulting in eternal oblivion. The concept of Ma'at permeated all aspects of Egyptian life, influencing their laws, social structures, and religious beliefs. Disrupting Ma'at was believed to bring chaos and imbalance, threatening the stability of the world. Her principles were not just legalistic, but also encompassed ethical and moral conduct, emphasizing the importance of honesty, compassion, and respect. Ma'at's connection to Thoth is significant, as he is often considered her child and associated with wisdom and knowledge, essential for upholding Ma'at. The Ankh, a symbol of life, is often associated with Ma'at, representing the eternal consequences of living in accordance with her principles. The concept of Ma'at is also related to the idea of universal balance, reflecting the interconnectedness of all things in the Egyptian worldview.

sources.title: The Egyptian Book of the Dead
sources.author: Various Scribes
